title: Legislation would require greater transit agency coordination leading to a more reliable, appealing experience for riders

raw_text:
```json
{
  "claims": [
    "Legislation would require greater transit agency coordination leading to a more reliable, appealing experience for riders.",
    "Senate Bill 917 would require transit agencies in the region to develop an integrated transit fare structure.",
    "Senate Bill 917 would create a Connected Network Plan to support schedule coordination and service standards.",
    "Senate Bill 917 would develop a single regional transit map and standardized wayfinding system.",
    "Currently, there are 27 independent transit agencies operating in the nine-county Bay Area.",
    "A lack of schedule coordination and real-time transit data across agencies can make transferring unreliable.",
    "Between 2001 and 2016, Bay Area bus speeds decreased by 9% while commute times increased by 11%.",
    "Only 12% of the population have used transit to commute since 1970.",
    "Between 2016 and 2018, ridership across Bay Area public transit systems fell by 5.2%.",
    "Regions with highly integrated networks of local and regional transit services, like Seattle or London, have consistently high levels of transit ridership.",
    "The integrated fare structure proposed by SB 917 will include no-cost local-local and local-regional transfers across the same and multiple transit agencies.",
    "SB 917 would further require the design of a single regional transit map and standardized wayfinding mechanisms.",
    "SB 917 would direct MTC and transit agencies to create a Connected Network Plan to identify a network of regional and local corridors that need service-level standards and improvements."
  ]
}
```
